content:
# Genesis Chapter 21 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 11v–12r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_021_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_021_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 16–17 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0020

---

*The Birth of Isaac — The LORD Visits Sarah (vv. 1–7)*

**21:1** And the LORD [וַיהוָה] visited [פָּקַד] Sarah [אֶת-שָׂרָה] as he had said [כַּאֲשֶׁר אָמָר] — and the LORD [וַיהוָה] did to Sarah [עָשָׂה לְשָׂרָה] as he had spoken [כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבֵּר].

**21:2** And Sarah conceived [וַתַּהַר שָׂרָה] and bore [וַתֵּלֶד] to Abraham a son [לְאַבְרָהָם בֵּן] in his old age [לִזְקֻנָיו] at the appointed time [לַמּוֹעֵד] that God had spoken to him [אֲשֶׁר-דִּבֶּר אֹתוֹ אֱלֹהִים].

**21:3** And Abraham called [וַיִּקְרָא אַבְרָהָם] the name [אֶת-שֵׁם] of his son [בְּנוֹ] who was born to him [הַנּוֹלַד-לוֹ] — whom Sarah bore to him [אֲשֶׁר-יָלְדָה-לּוֹ שָׂרָה] — Isaac [יִצְחָק].

**21:4** And Abraham circumcised [וַיָּמָל אַבְרָהָם] Isaac his son [אֶת-יִצְחָק בְּנוֹ] — [a] son of eight days [בֶּן-שְׁמֹנַת יָמִים] — as God had commanded him [כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה אֹתוֹ אֱלֹהִים].

**21:5** And Abraham [וְאַבְרָהָם] was a son of a hundred years [בֶּן-מְאַת שָׁנָה] at the being born to him [בְּהִוָּלֶד לוֹ] — Isaac his son [אֵת יִצְחָק בְּנוֹ].

**21:6** And Sarah said [וַתֹּאמֶר שָׂרָה]: Laughter [צְחֹק] — God has made for me [עָשָׂה לִי אֱלֹהִים] — all who hear [כָּל-הַשֹּׁמֵעַ] will laugh [יִצְחַק] with me [לִי].

**21:7** And she said [וַתֹּאמֶר]: Who would have said [מִי מִלֵּל] to Abraham [לְאַבְרָהָם] — Sarah has nursed sons [הֵינִיקָה בָנִים שָׂרָה]? For I have borne a son [כִּי-יָלַדְתִּי בֵן] in his old age [לִזְקֻנָיו].

---

*The Weaning Feast — Sarah Demands the Expulsion of Hagar and Ishmael (vv. 8–13)*

**21:8** And the child grew [וַיִּגְדַּל הַיֶּלֶד] and was weaned [וַיִּגָּמַל] and Abraham made a great feast [וַיַּעַשׂ אַבְרָהָם מִשְׁתֶּה גָדוֹל] on the day of the weaning [בְּיוֹם הִגָּמֵל] of Isaac [אֶת-יִצְחָק].

**21:9** And Sarah saw [וַתֵּרֶא שָׂרָה] the son of Hagar the Egyptian [אֶת-בֶּן-הָגָר הַמִּצְרִית] — whom she had borne [אֲשֶׁר-יָלְדָה] to Abraham [לְאַבְרָהָם] — laughing [מְצַחֵק].

**21:10** And she said [וַתֹּאמֶר] to Abraham [לְאַבְרָהָם]: Drive out [גָּרֵשׁ] this slave woman [הָאָמָה הַזֹּאת] and her son [וְאֶת-בְּנָהּ] — for the son of this slave woman [כִּי לֹא יִירַשׁ בֶּן-הָאָמָה הַזֹּאת] shall not inherit [לֹא יִירַשׁ] with my son [עִם-בְּנִי] — with Isaac [עִם-יִצְחָק].

**21:11** And the matter was very grievous [וַיֵּרַע הַדָּבָר מְאֹד] in the eyes of Abraham [בְּעֵינֵי אַבְרָהָם] on account of [עַל] his son [בְּנוֹ].

**21:12** And God said [וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים] to Abraham [אֶל-אַבְרָהָם]: Let it not be grievous [אַל-יֵרַע] in your eyes [בְּעֵינֶיךָ] on account of the boy [עַל-הַנַּעַר] and on account of your slave woman [וְעַל-אֲמָתֶךָ] — all that Sarah says to you [כֹּל אֲשֶׁר תֹּאמַר אֵלֶיךָ שָׂרָה] — hear her voice [שְׁמַע בְּקֹלָהּ] — for through Isaac [כִּי בְיִצְחָק] shall offspring be called to you [יִקָּרֵא לְךָ זָרַע].

**21:13** And also the son of the slave woman [וְגַם אֶת-בֶּן-הָאָמָה] — into a nation [לְגוֹי] I will set him [אֲשִׂימֶנּוּ] — for your offspring he is [כִּי זַרְעֲךָ הוּא].

---

*Hagar and Ishmael in the Wilderness of Beer-sheba (vv. 14–21)*

**21:14** And Abraham rose early in the morning [וַיַּשְׁכֵּם אַבְרָהָם בַּבֹּקֶר] and took bread [וַיִּקַּח-לֶחֶם] and a skin of water [וְחֵמַת מַיִם] and gave [them] to Hagar [וַיִּתֵּן אֶל-הָגָר] — placing [them] on her shoulder [שָׂם עַל-שִׁכְמָהּ] — and the child [וְאֶת-הַיֶּלֶד] — and he sent her away [וַיְשַׁלְּחֶהָ] — and she went [וַתֵּלֶךְ] and wandered [וַתֵּתַע] in the wilderness of Beer-sheba [בְּמִדְבַּר בְּאֵר שָׁבַע].

**21:15** And the water was spent [וַיִּכְלוּ הַמַּיִם] from the skin [מִן-הַחֵמֶת] and she cast the child [וַתַּשְׁלֵךְ אֶת-הַיֶּלֶד] under one of the shrubs [תַּחַת אַחַד הַשִּׂיחִם].

**21:16** And she went [וַתֵּלֶךְ] and sat down [וַתֵּשֶׁב] opposite him [לָהּ מִנֶּגֶד] — at a distance [הַרְחֵק] as it were a bowshot [כִּמְטַחֲוֵי קֶשֶׁת] — for she said [כִּי אָמְרָה]: Let me not see [אַל-אֶרְאֶה] the death of the child [בְּמוֹת הַיֶּלֶד] — and she sat down [וַתֵּשֶׁב] opposite [מִנֶּגֶד] and lifted up her voice [וַתִּשָּׂא אֶת-קֹלָהּ] and wept [וַתֵּבְךְּ].

**21:17** And God heard [וַיִּשְׁמַע אֱלֹהִים] the voice of the boy [אֶת-קוֹל הַנַּעַר] and the angel of God [וַיִּקְרָא מַלְאַךְ אֱלֹהִים] called [וַיִּקְרָא] to Hagar [אֶל-הָגָר] from the heavens [מִן-הַשָּׁמַיִם] and said to her [וַיֹּאמֶר לָהּ]: What is wrong with you [מַה-לָּךְ הָגָר] — Hagar? Do not fear [אַל-תִּירְאִי] — for God has heard [כִּי-שָׁמַע אֱלֹהִים] the voice of the boy [אֶל-קוֹל הַנַּעַר] where he is [בַּאֲשֶׁר הוּא-שָׁם].

**21:18** Rise up [קוּמִי] — lift up the boy [שְׂאִי אֶת-הַנַּעַר] and hold him [וְהַחֲזִיקִי אֶת-יָדֵךְ בּוֹ] by your hand — for into a great nation [כִּי-לְגוֹי גָּדוֹל] I will set him [אֲשִׂימֶנּוּ].

**21:19** And God opened [וַיִּפְקַח אֱלֹהִים] her eyes [אֶת-עֵינֶיהָ] and she saw [וַתֵּרֶא] a well of water [בְּאֵר מָיִם] and she went [וַתֵּלֶךְ] and filled the skin with water [וַתְּמַלֵּא אֶת-הַחֵמֶת מַיִם] and gave [the boy] to drink [וַתַּשְׁקְ אֶת-הַנַּעַר].

**21:20** And God was with the boy [וַיְהִי אֱלֹהִים אֶת-הַנַּעַר] and he grew [וַיִּגְדַּל] and he dwelt in the wilderness [וַיֵּשֶׁב בַּמִּדְבָּר] and became [וַיְהִי] a shooter of the bow [רֹבֶה קַשָּׁת].

**21:21** And he dwelt [וַיֵּשֶׁב] in the wilderness of Paran [בְּמִדְבַּר פָּארָן] and his mother took for him [וַתִּקַּח-לוֹ אִמּוֹ] a wife [אִשָּׁה] from the land of Egypt [מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם].

---

*Abimelech's Covenant with Abraham at Beer-sheba (vv. 22–34)*

**21:22** And it came about [וַיְהִי] at that time [בָּעֵת הַהִוא] that Abimelech spoke [וַיֹּאמֶר אֲבִימֶלֶךְ] — and Phicol [וּפִיכֹל] the commander of his army [שַׂר-צְבָאוֹ] — to Abraham [אֶל-אַבְרָהָם] saying [לֵאמֹר]: God is with you [אֱלֹהִים עִמְּךָ] in all that you do [בְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר-אַתָּה עֹשֶׂה].

**21:23** And now [וְעַתָּה] swear to me [הִשָּׁבְעָה לִּי] here [הֵנָּה] by God [בֵּאלֹהִים] — that you will not deal falsely with me [אִם-תִּשְׁקֹר לִי] and with my offspring [וּלְנִינִי] and with my descendants [וּלְנֶכְדִּי] — according to the loyalty [כַּחֶסֶד] that I have done with you [אֲשֶׁר-עָשִׂיתִי עִמְּךָ] — you shall do with me [תַּעֲשֶׂה עִמָּדִי] and with the land [וְעִם-הָאָרֶץ] in which you have sojourned [אֲשֶׁר-גַּרְתָּה בָּהּ].

**21:24** And Abraham said [וַיֹּאמֶר אַבְרָהָם]: I will swear [אָנֹכִי אִשָּׁבֵעַ].

**21:25** And Abraham reproved [וְהוֹכִיחַ אַבְרָהָם] Abimelech [אֶת-אֲבִימֶלֶךְ] on account of the well of water [עַל-אֹדוֹת בְּאֵר הַמַּיִם] that the servants of Abimelech had seized [אֲשֶׁר גָּזְלוּ עַבְדֵי אֲבִימֶלֶךְ].

**21:26** And Abimelech said [וַיֹּאמֶר אֲבִימֶלֶךְ]: I do not know [לֹא יָדַעְתִּי] who did this thing [מִי עָשָׂה אֶת-הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה] — and also you [וְגַם אַתָּה] did not tell me [לֹא-הִגַּדְתָּ לִּי] and also I [וְגַם אָנֹכִי] — I did not hear [לֹא שָׁמַעְתִּי] except today [בִּלְתִּי הַיּוֹם].

**21:27** And Abraham took [וַיִּקַּח אַבְרָהָם] sheep and cattle [צֹאן וּבָקָר] and gave [them] to Abimelech [וַיִּתֵּן לַאֲבִימֶלֶךְ] and the two of them cut [וַיִּכְרְתוּ שְׁנֵיהֶם] a covenant [בְּרִית].

**21:28** And Abraham set apart [וַיַּצֵּב אַבְרָהָם] seven ewe-lambs [אֶת-שֶׁבַע כִּבְשֹׂת הַצֹּאן] of the flock [הַצֹּאן] by themselves [לְבַדְּהֶן].

**21:29** And Abimelech said [וַיֹּאמֶר אֲבִימֶלֶךְ] to Abraham [אֶל-אַבְרָהָם]: What are these seven ewe-lambs [מָה הֵנָּה שֶׁבַע כְּבָשֹׂת הָאֵלֶּה] that you have set apart [אֲשֶׁר הִצַּבְתָּ] by themselves [לְבַדְּהֶן]?

**21:30** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: For [כִּי] the seven ewe-lambs [אֶת-שֶׁבַע כְּבָשֹׂת] you shall take from my hand [תִּקַּח מִיָּדִי] — in order that [בַּעֲבוּר] it may be for me a witness [תִּהְיֶה-לִּי לְעֵדָה] that I dug this well [כִּי חָפַרְתִּי אֶת-הַבְּאֵר הַזֹּאת].

**21:31** Therefore [עַל-כֵּן] he called that place [קָרָא לַמָּקוֹם הַהוּא] Beer-sheba [בְּאֵר שָׁבַע] — for there [כִּי שָׁם] the two of them swore an oath [נִשְׁבְּעוּ שְׁנֵיהֶם].

**21:32** And they cut a covenant [וַיִּכְרְתוּ בְרִית] at Beer-sheba [בִּבְאֵר שָׁבַע] and Abimelech rose [וַיָּקָם אֲבִימֶלֶךְ] and Phicol [וּפִיכֹל] the commander of his army [שַׂר-צְבָאוֹ] and they returned [וַיָּשֻׁבוּ] to the land of the Philistines [אֶל-אֶרֶץ פְּלִשְׁתִּים].

**21:33** And [Abraham] planted [וַיִּטַּע] a tamarisk tree [אֶשֶׁל] at Beer-sheba [בִּבְאֵר שָׁבַע] and he called there [וַיִּקְרָא-שָׁם] on the name [בְּשֵׁם] of the LORD [יְהוָה] — the Everlasting God [אֵל עוֹלָם].

**21:34** And Abraham sojourned [וַיָּגָר אַבְרָהָם] in the land of the Philistines [בְּאֶרֶץ פְּלִשְׁתִּים] many days [יָמִים רַבִּים].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**On the tsechoq / yitschaq (צְחֹק / יִצְחָק) wordplay — the governing motif of the chapter:**
The name *Yitschaq* (יִצְחָק, "Isaac") is derived from the root *ts-ch-q* (צ-ח-ק), meaning "to laugh." This chapter is saturated with the root: (a) v. 3 — the name *Yitschaq* is given; (b) v. 6 — *tsechoq* (צְחֹק, "laughter") — "God has made laughter for me"; (c) v. 6 — *yitschaq* (יִצְחַק, "will laugh") — "all who hear will laugh with me"; (d) v. 9 — *metsacheq* (מְצַחֵק, "laughing/playing/mocking") — Ishmael doing something with this root word. The entire narrative from Gen 17–21 hinges on the transformation of *tsechoq* from a laugh of disbelief (17:17, Abraham; 18:12, Sarah) to a laugh of joy (21:6). I have rendered every instance of the root literally with a consistent English derivative, preserving this network of meaning.

**21:1 — "visited" (*pāqad*, פָּקַד):**
The verb *pāqad* (פָּקַד) carries a dense semantic range: to visit, attend to, appoint, muster, remember, oversee. It is used of divine action that changes a person's condition — usually for the better here (though it can mean "punish" in other contexts). The subject is *YHWH*, and the object is Sarah. The theological weight is deliberate: God remembered his promise and acted upon it. The LXX Vaticanus reads *epeskepsato kyrios tēn Sarran* (ἐπεσκέψατο κύριος τὴν Σαρραν), using *episkeptomai* (to visit, oversee), the standard Greek rendering of *pāqad*. I render it "visited."

**21:1 — Double statement of fulfillment:**
V. 1 contains two parallel clauses: "as he had said... as he had spoken." The first uses *'āmar* (אָמַר, the ordinary verb for speech) and the second *dibber* (דִּבֶּר, the more formal/weighty verb for declaring). The repetition of the fulfillment formula is emphatic — the narrator insists twice that what happened was exactly what was promised. This is a deliberate literary flourish, not redundancy.

**21:2 — "at the appointed time" (*lammô'ēd*, לַמּוֹעֵד):**
*Mô'ēd* (מוֹעֵד) means an appointed time, a fixed meeting, an assembly. It recurs from Gen 18:14 ("at the appointed time I will return to you"). The word carries covenant resonance — God's word sets an appointment and keeps it. The LXX renders it *kata ton kairon* (κατὰ τὸν καιρόν), "according to the season/time."

**21:3 — "who was born to him — whom Sarah bore to him" — double relative:**
The Hebrew repeats the relative clause — *hannôlad-lô... 'ăšer-yālĕdāh-llô śārāh* — first as a passive participle ("the one born to him") then as a finite verb clause ("whom Sarah bore to him"). The first establishes paternity; the second establishes maternity. In the context of a narrative where the identity of the true heir is at stake, this double genealogical affirmation is not stylistic padding but substantive insistence.

**21:5 — "a son of a hundred years" (*ben-mĕ'at šānāh*):**
The Hebrew idiom *ben* + time expression = "one who is [X] years old" (literally "a son of a hundred years"). This construction is idiomatic and I have retained it literally rather than smoothing to "one hundred years old," since the "son of" construction recurs throughout Genesis to mark birth records and genealogies.

**21:6 — "Laughter — God has made for me" (*tsechoq 'āśāh lî 'ĕlōhîm*):**
The word *tsechoq* (צְחֹק) stands at the very head of Sarah's speech — fronted, emphatic, isolated. The word order in Hebrew is: Laughter — God — has made — for me. I have preserved this fronted position. The second clause — *kol-haššômēa' yitschaq lî* — is ambiguous: does *lî* mean "with me" (sharing my joy) or "at me" (laughing in mockery or disbelief)? The preposition *lî* can sustain both. I render it "with me," as the context is Sarah's joy, but the echo of the earlier laughter-of-disbelief haunts the phrase. The LXX Vaticanus reads *hos ean akousē, synchareitai moi* (ὃς ἐὰν ἀκούσῃ, συγχαρεῖταί μοι), "whoever hears will rejoice with me" — choosing joy and removing the Isaac/laugh pun, but confirming the "with me" reading.

**21:7 — "Who would have said" (*mî millēl*):**
The verb *millēl* (מִלֵּל) is an archaic Piel of an otherwise rare root — found only here in the Pentateuch in this form. It conveys speaking or declaring. The LXX renders it *tis anangeleî* (τίς ἀναγγελεῖ), "who will announce/declare." Sarah speaks in the 3rd person about herself — "Sarah has nursed sons" — a rhetorical distancing that emphasizes the inconceivability of the event.

**21:9 — metsacheq (מְצַחֵק) — the critical crux:**
This is one of the most discussed words in Genesis. Ishmael is seen *metsacheq* (מְצַחֵק) — the Piel participle of *ts-ch-q* (the Isaac root). In the Piel, this verb can mean: (a) laughing/rejoicing; (b) playing (as a child); (c) mocking/scorning; (d) sexual activity (Gen 26:8; 39:14, 17). The ambiguity is almost certainly intentional — the narrator uses the very root of Isaac's name to describe what Ishmael is doing, creating irony or threat or rivalry or all three simultaneously. I have rendered it "laughing," the most literal rendering, with this note flagging the full range. **LXX Vaticanus diverges critically here:** the LXX adds *meta Isaak tou huiou autēs* (μετὰ Ισαακ τοῦ υἱοῦ αὐτῆς), "with Isaac her son" — a reading that shapes interpretation toward innocent play and was influential in patristic exegesis. This phrase is *absent* from the Masoretic Hebrew text I read in the Leningrad Codex on folio 11v. Paul's reading in Gal 4:29 ("he who was born according to the flesh persecuted him who was born according to the Spirit") draws on a tradition of *metsacheq* as mockery/persecution.

**21:10 — "slave woman" (*'āmāh*, אָמָה):**
Sarah uses the term *'āmāh* (אָמָה, female slave) throughout this passage — never Hagar's name, never "my servant." This dehumanizing register is in the Hebrew and I have preserved it. Compare the narrator's use of *šiphchāh* (שִׁפְחָה, maidservant/concubine) for Hagar in 16:1-3, and *'āmāh* here and at vv. 12-13. God also uses *'āmāh* at v. 12-13, picking up Sarah's register. The distinction between these two words for female slave is itself a scholarly discussion; I have rendered both as "slave woman" throughout this chapter.

**21:12 — "through Isaac shall offspring be called to you" (*kî bĕyitschāq yiqqārē' lĕkā zāra'*):**
This sentence is among the most theologically loaded in Genesis. The verb *yiqqārē'* (יִקָּרֵא) is Niphal passive: "[your] offspring will be called/named through Isaac." The meaning is that the line of promise narrows through Isaac specifically — Ishmael is Abraham's seed but not the seed of promise. Paul cites the Septuagint version of this verse at Rom 9:7 and Gal 4:28 as a key text for his theology of election. The LXX Vaticanus reads *en Isaak klēthēsetai soi sperma* (ἐν Ισαακ κληθήσεταί σοι σπέρμα), faithfully rendering the Hebrew.

**21:14 — "placing [them] on her shoulder" — syntactic difficulty:**
The Hebrew of v. 14 presents a classic syntactic problem. After "gave to Hagar," the text reads *śām 'al-šikmāh* (שָׂם עַל-שִׁכְמָהּ) — "placing on her shoulder" — with no expressed subject. The antecedent of *śām* must be Abraham (continuing from the main clause) but the construction is abrupt. I have left the implied subject in brackets — "[placing them]" — rather than inserting a full "Abraham" that does not appear at this point. More troubling is the question of *what* is placed on her shoulder: only the bread and water, or also the child? The later tradition in LXX and in rabbinic reading differs. The Hebrew *wĕ'et-hayyeled* ("and the child") follows the shoulder-placing clause — it is ambiguous whether the child too is placed on her shoulder or whether "and the child" is simply a second direct object of the giving (i.e., Abraham gave Hagar the provisions AND sent the child away with her). I have preserved the Hebrew order without resolving this.

**21:15 — "she cast the child" (*watašlēk 'et-hayyeled*):**
The verb *šālak* (שָׁלַךְ, Hiphil) is strong: "cast, throw, fling." The LXX uses *erripsen* (ἔρριψεν), equally strong — "hurled." This is not gentle placement. The act conveys Hagar's extremity of despair. I retain "cast" over the softer "placed" found in some translations.

**21:16 — "as it were a bowshot" (*kimitachăwê qešet*):**
This rare expression *kimitachăwê qešet* (כִּמְטַחֲוֵי קֶשֶׁת) occurs only here in the Hebrew Bible. It literally means "as the stretching/shooting of a bow" — the distance an arrow flies. The exact range is uncertain (perhaps 100-300 meters). The very rarity of the word underscores the narrator's care in depicting the precise, agonizing distance Hagar chose: close enough to hear, far enough not to watch. The LXX renders it *hōsei toxou bolēn* (ὡσεὶ τόξου βολήν), "as if a bowshot," confirming the sense.

**21:17 — "God heard the voice of the boy" — Ishmael's name fulfilled:**
The name *Yišmā'ēl* (יִשְׁמָעֵאל) means "God hears" (*yišma'* + *'ēl*). V. 17 enacts the name: *wayyišma' 'ĕlōhîm 'et-qôl hanna'ar* — "and God heard the voice of the boy." The angel then explains to Hagar: *šāma' 'ĕlōhîm* — "God has heard." The name named in 16:11 is here confirmed in event. Note that it is the *boy's* voice God hears, not Hagar's weeping (v. 16), though Hagar is the one who wept. The Masoretic text says the angel calls *to Hagar*, from heaven — a voice from the heavens addressing a woman in the desert, a striking theophanic form.

**21:19 — "God opened her eyes" (*wayyipqach 'ĕlōhîm 'et-'ênêhā*):**
The verb *pāqach* (פָּקַח) means to open (eyes or ears). The well was apparently already there; what changed was Hagar's ability to perceive it. The divine opening of eyes is significant: compare 3:7 (Adam and Eve's eyes opened), 2 Kgs 6:17 (Elisha's servant's eyes opened to see the heavenly army). The well was real, not a miracle of creation — the miracle was perception. I render the phrase plainly without harmonizing comment.

**21:20 — "a shooter of the bow" (*rōbeh qaššāt*):**
The Masoretic text reads *rōbeh qaššāt* (רֹבֶה קַשָּׁת) — literally "archer of the bow" or "one who draws/shoots the bow." The LXX renders it *toxotēs* (τοξότης), "archer." This brief notice of Ishmael's vocation fulfills the oracle of Gen 16:12 — "he shall be a wild donkey of a man, his hand against everyone." The bow of the wilderness hunter is his instrument.

**21:22 — Phicol (פִּיכֹל):**
The name *Pîkōl* (פִּיכֹל) appears here and at Gen 26:26. Its etymology is uncertain; some suggest it means "strong of mouth" or "all the mouths" in a Semitic language, but the name may be foreign (Philistine? Anatolian?). The LXX renders it *Phichol* (Φικολ), a simple transliteration. I render it "Phicol" per the conventional English form.

**21:23 — "my offspring... my descendants" (*nînî ûnekdî*):**
The Hebrew *nîn* (נִין) and *neked* (נֶכֶד) are rare words meaning something like "son/offspring" and "grandson/descendant" respectively. The pair forms a hendiadys for "my posterity for generations to come." The LXX renders them *tois teknois mou kai tois ekgonois mou* (τοῖς τέκνοις μου καὶ τοῖς ἐκγόνοις μου), "my children and my offspring." I have rendered them literally as "my offspring and my descendants," preserving the generational pair without conflating them.

**21:25 — "reproved" (*hôkîach*, הוֹכִיחַ):**
The Hiphil of *yākach* (יָכַח) means to reprove, correct, adjudicate, dispute. Abraham formally registers a complaint or dispute with Abimelech regarding the seized well. The same verb root appears at 20:16 (*nōkāḥat*, "she was reproved/vindicated"). I render it "reproved" throughout to preserve the lexical link.

**21:31 — Beer-sheba etymology (*bĕ'ēr šāba'*):**
The text explains the name *Bĕ'ēr Šāba'* (בְּאֵר שָׁבַע) by the oath (*nišbĕ'û*, "they swore") — "for there the two of them swore." The name sustains a deliberate double meaning: *bĕ'ēr* (בְּאֵר, "well") + *šāba'* (שֶׁבַע) which can mean either *šāba'* (שָׁבַע, "seven" — hence the seven ewe-lambs of vv. 28-30) or *šāba'* from the root *š-b-*' (שׁ-ב-ע, "to swear an oath" — hence the oath of v. 23 and 31). The name thus means simultaneously "well of seven" and "well of the oath." Both meanings are structurally present in the narrative (seven lambs and the sworn covenant), and both are enshrined in the name. The narrator achieves this with a single toponym. The LXX renders it *Phrear tou horkou* (Φρέαρ τοῦ ὅρκου), "well of the oath" — choosing only one of the two meanings.

**21:33 — "a tamarisk tree" (*'ēšel*, אֶשֶׁל) and "the Everlasting God" (*'Ēl 'Ôlām*, אֵל עוֹלָם):**
The *'ēšel* (אֶשֶׁל) is variously identified as a tamarisk tree (*Tamarix*) or as a grove/garden. The tamarisk is a hardy desert tree known for longevity and deep roots — appropriate for marking a covenantal site. The LXX renders *'ēšel* as *aroura* (ἄρουρα), "field" or "cultivated land," which diverges from the arboreal meaning of the Hebrew. The divine epithet *'Ēl 'Ôlām* (אֵל עוֹלָם, "God Everlasting" or "the Everlasting God") appears here for the first and only time in Genesis. The word *'ôlām* (עוֹלָם) means "age, eternity, everlasting duration" — God is invoked as the God of unlimited time, the eternal one. This title is likely connected to the ancient sanctuary at Beer-sheba where this name was worshiped. The LXX renders it *theos aiōnios* (θεὸς αἰώνιος), "eternal God," a straightforward equivalent. I render it "the Everlasting God" to preserve the adjectival force of the epithet. Note that this is the only occurrence in the chapter where Abraham himself invokes the divine name — as opposed to God speaking or the narrator referring to God.

**21:34 — "sojourned" (*wayyāgār*):**
The same verb *gûr* (גּוּר, "to sojourn as an alien") used of Abraham in Gen 20:1 at Gerar closes the chapter here — "Abraham sojourned in the land of the Philistines." The verb marks Abraham's perpetual status as a landless sojourner even in the midst of covenant-making and naming of holy sites. He never owns the land; he lives in it as a resident foreigner.

**LXX Vaticanus Observations (Pages 16–17):**
The Vaticanus pages 16-17 are clearly readable. The chapter division marker *CAP. XXIII* (visible at the top of page 16) represents the older Greek chapter enumeration. The most significant LXX variant in this chapter is at v. 9, where the LXX adds *meta Isaak tou huiou autēs* ("with Isaac her son") — absent from the Masoretic Hebrew. This expansion was almost certainly added to clarify *metsacheq* as innocent play (with Isaac), thereby removing the threatening ambiguity of the Hebrew. At v. 6 the LXX interprets the ambiguous *lî* as "with me" (*synchareitai moi*). At v. 33 the LXX renders *'ēšel* as "field" rather than "tree." At v. 31 the LXX chooses "well of the oath" over "well of seven." At v. 16, the LXX reads "far off" and preserves the bowshot idiom (*hōsĕi toxou bolēn*). Overall the Vaticanus LXX for this chapter is a competent and generally close rendering of the Hebrew, with a handful of expansions and interpretive glosses.

**DSS Note:**
Per the chapter manifest, no significant Dead Sea Scrolls fragments are extant for Genesis 21. The Leningrad Codex remains the sole primary Hebrew witness for this chapter.
